definition: A way of ensuring that only authorised users (or automated systems) can access data or services.
Temporarily suspending an account, in response to the detection of unusual/suspicious activity.
A set of NCSC services that are designed to reduce high-volume commodity cyber attacks.
notes: also known as: acd; mail check; web check | see also: acd; mail check; web check

definition: A person who manages a computer system, usually with highly privileged access to data and services.

Artificial intelligence (AI) describes computer systems which can perform tasks usually requiring human intelligence. This could include visual perception, speech recognition or translation between languages.
notes: also known as: artificial intelligence; machine learning | see also: artificial intelligence; machine learning

definition: A security strategy organisations can use that approves a list of email addresses, IP addresses, domain names or applications, while denying all others, to protect computers and networks from threats.
notes: also known as: whitelist whitelisting and blacklisting is still used across the industry; but the ncsc do not use these terms. read more why in our blog post: /blog-post/terminology-its-not-black-and-white; deny list | see also: whitelist whitelisting and blacklisting is still used across the industry; but the ncsc do not use these terms. read more why in our blog post: /blog-post/terminology-its-not-black-and-white; deny list

definition: Google's mobile operating system, used by several smartphone and tablet manufacturers.
Software that is designed to detect, stop and remove malicious software (malware).
Software that is designed to detect, stop and remove viruses and other kinds of malicious software.
notes: also known as: av; anti-malware | see also: av; anti-malware

definition: A software program running on a computer. Usually referred to as an 'app' when running on a smartphone or tablet.
Advanced persistent threat. A nation-state or highly capable criminal actor that gains unauthorised access to a system and remains undetected for a long time.
notes: also known as: advanced persistent threat; hacker | see also: advanced persistent threat; hacker

definition: Artificial intelligence (AI) describes computer systems which can perform tasks usually requiring human intelligence. This could include visual perception, speech recognition or translation between languages.
notes: also known as: ai; machine intelligence; machine learning | see also: ai; machine intelligence; machine learning

definition: Identifying and recording of an organisation's physical assets, software, data, essential staff and utilities.
A systematic method for analysing and depicting the different ways a computer system might be attacked.
The method an attacker uses to gain unauthorised access to a computer system or device.
Individual or group that conduct cyber attacks.
notes: also known as: threat actor; cyber attack | see also: threat actor; cyber attack

definition: The process of determining if someone (or something) is who (or what) it claims to be.
An option in Windows to automatically run programs contained on removable media when inserted into the device.
A copy of computer data, usually stored in the cloud or on removable media, that can be used to recover from data loss or theft.
Border Gateway Protocol. A protocol that exchanges routing information across the internet, typically between ISPs or large organisations.
notes: also known as: border gateway protocol; isp | see also: border gateway protocol; isp

definition: A type of authentication based on biological data that is unique and specific to a person (such as a fingerprint or face ID).
Basic input-output system. The firmware built into PCs that connect the vital components, now superseded by UEFI.
notes: also known as: basic input-output system; uefi | see also: basic input-output system; uefi

definition: A short-range, wireless means of communicating between devices (mobile phones, tablets, computers).
A distributed network of infected devices on the internet, used to commit co-ordinated cyber attacks without their owner's knowledge.
An incident in which data, computer systems or networks are accessed or affected without authorisation.
An application that displays information and accesses services from the web. Edge, Safari, Firefox and Chrome are all browsers.
notes: also known as: web browser; www | see also: web browser; www

definition: An attack that uses computing power to automatically enter a large number of guesses for security credentials in an attempt to gain unauthorised access.
Term used to describe large quantities of (often personal) data that needs special protection.
Bring your own device. When employees use their own device(s) for work purposes, but the employer owns corporate data and resources that may be accessed or stored on it.
notes: also known as: bring your own device

definition: Certificate authority. An organisation that issues digital certificates to allow the authentication and secure exchange of data
An organisation that offers communication services over land-wire, sea cable, mobile, and/or satellite systems.
notes: also known as: isp | see also: isp

definition: Competent authorities. The organisations responsible for enforcing regulation under the NIS Directive.
A form of digital identity for a computer, user or organisation to allow the authentication and secure exchange of data.
A form of digital identity for a computer, user or organisation to allow the authentication and secure exchange of data.
An artificial intelligence language model developed by OpenAI designed to mimic human conversation, based on the input it receives.
notes: see also: ai; large language model; llm; artificial intelligence

definition: An on-demand, massively scalable service, hosted on shared infrastructure, accessible via the internet. Typical services include providing data storage, data processing, and pre-built functionality, such as logging.
Content Management System. Software used to create, manage and publish digital content, usually to the web.
notes: also known as: content management system

definition: Processes for defining and maintaining the consistency of configurations of software, hardware and other elements of an organisation to ensure reliable performance through its life.
A method where attackers use passwords stolen from one web service to try and access accounts on another service, taking advantage of any reuse of username and password combinations.
Information used to verify someone's identity, such as a password, token or certificate, that authenticates use of a service.
National assets that are essential for the functioning of society, such as those associated with energy supply, water supply, transportation, health and telecommunications.
Technologies primarily based on maths and computer science for protecting information so that it is only accessible to authorised recipients.
Cyber security incident response team. An expert group that manages the response to a cyber security event or incident.
notes: also known as: computer security incident response team

definition: A tool for assessing how well suppliers of essential services are managing cyber security risks under the NIS Directive.
An attempt to damage, disrupt or gain unauthorised access to computer systems, networks or devices.
notes: also known as: hack incident; attacker; cyber threat; hacker; insider threat | see also: hack incident; attacker; cyber threat; hacker; insider threat

definition: A government-backed, industry supported scheme to help organisations protect themselves against common cyber attacks.
The protection of devices, services and networks - and the information on them - from unauthorised access, theft or damage.
A long-term plan of action with the aim of implementing cyber security.
The threat of a cyber attack to a user or organisation, and the unauthorised access, theft or damage that could result.
Scheme run by the NCSC that encourage children and young adults to consider a career in cyber security.
Data in a persistent state such as when stored on hard disks, removable media or backups.
A managed facility that houses data storage and computer systems.
Data as it is transferred from one location to another, either through a private network or the internet.
Distributed Denial of Service. A type of cyber attack where a computer service is overloaded by requests from multiple computers or locations, so that real users can no longer access the service.
notes: also known as: distributed denial of service; botnet; dos | see also: distributed denial of service; botnet; dos

definition: Making information that has been encrypted readable again.
A security control that organisations can put in place which blocks their users from connecting to or accessing named entities (such as IP addresses or domains) that may be suspicious.
notes: also known as: blacklist whitelisting and blacklisting is still used across the industry; but the ncsc do not use these terms. read more about the decision in the blog post - 'terminology: it's not all black and white'; allow list | see also: blacklist whitelisting and blacklisting is still used across the industry; but the ncsc do not use these terms. read more about the decision in the blog post - 'terminology: it's not all black and white'; allow list

definition: Computer-based hardware that physically exists, such as a desktop computer, smartphone or tablet.
A type of brute-force attack where words in a dictionary, phrases or common passwords are used to guess credentials.
The traces of digital information or 'footprint' that a person's online activity leaves behind.
The provision of services (such as banking or online shopping) via the internet, or an electronic network.
A technique used to validate the authenticity and integrity of a message, software or digital document.
DomainKeys Identified Mail. An email authentication method that adds a digital signature to every outgoing message, to verify the origin of the message.
Direct Memory Access. Allows devices to access main memory outside of the control of the CPU, allowing faster data rates
Domain-based Messaging and Reporting Conformance. An email authentication and reporting protocol that helps to monitor and improve protection from fraudulent email.
Demilitarised zone. A controlled segment of an organisation's private network (which typically separates it from the internet), used to host their publicly accessible services such as websites.
notes: also known as: demilitarised zone; firewall | see also: demilitarised zone; firewall

definition: Domain Name System. The directory service that translates internet domain names (for example www.bbc.co.uk) to their corresponding IP addresses.
notes: also known as: domain name service; ip address | see also: domain name service; ip address

definition: A set of network addresses on the internet, managed by an organisation or person.
Denial of Service. A type of cyber attack where a computer service is overloaded, so that real users can no longer access the service.
notes: also known as: denial of service; ddos | see also: denial of service; ddos

definition: A type of cyber attack where the victim unintentionally installs malware (such as viruses) on to a device, without the owner's knowledge.
notes: also known as: drive-by download; malware | see also: drive-by download; malware

definition: The NCSC provides cyber security guidance and resources for the education sector to help protect everyone from schools to universities.
notes: also known as: research & academia; research & academia | see also: research & academia; research & academia

definition: Protection of information by making it unreadable by everyone except those with the key to decrypt it.
Term to collectively describe modern smartphones, laptops and tablets that connect to an organisation's network.
notes: also known as: eud; mdm | see also: eud; mdm

definition: Any large business or organisation that uses computer systems, networks and devices.
Equality, Diversity and Inclusion
A trusted third party service that holds money, software or other assets pending completion of a transaction.
The networking technology that uses wires (rather than Wi-Fi) to connect devices to networks (including the internet).
Cyber incident exercising helps organisations to establish how resilient they are to cyber attack, and practice their response in a safe environment.
Development of tools to take advantage of a vulnerability in a system to gain unauthorised access or produce unwanted effects
Fast IDentity Online 2 is a set of standards that enable authentication using cryptographic keys instead of passwords.
A biometric device that scans fingerprints to authenticate users.
Hardware or software used to prevent unauthorised access to or from a network.
The software embedded into a device that allows it to function.
Networking appliance that allows data to flow between different types of networks.
Someone who uses their technology skills to break into computers, systems and networks.
Converting data into a fixed-length unreadable format that can't easily be reversed. Commonly used to protect passwords.
Infrastructure (such as a web server) designed to attract attackers in order to learn how better to defend systems.
A network endpoint. For example, a PC, laptop, smartphone or tablet.
The physical location within the range of a wifi access point that provides access to the internet.
Computer software or hardware that can run and manage one or more virtual machines.
Industrial Control System. Systems, often involved with critical national infrastructure, which control and respond to physical feedback.
notes: also known as: industrial control system; critical national infrastructure (cni) | see also: industrial control system; critical national infrastructure (cni)

definition: An organisation (such as Google, Microsoft and Facebook) that provides online user identification on behalf of third parties.
Activities to identify, analyse, and determine the response to cyber security incidents, to minimise the immediate and long-term business impact.
Managing the risks to data, networks and information systems
notes: also known as: ia; risk management | see also: ia; risk management

definition: A business model where consumers access virtualised hardware and software resources over the internet.
notes: also known as: iaas; platform as a service (paas); software as a service (saas) | see also: iaas; platform as a service (paas); software as a service (saas)

definition: Deliberate or accidental threat to an organisation's security from someone who has authorised access (such as an employee).
The global 'network of networks' that provides a vast range of services such as the world wide web, email, social media and streaming services.
Technology describing everyday objects (rather than computers and smartphones) that can connect to the internet. Examples include speakers, televisions and security cameras.
Internet Protocol.  A communications protocol that relays data over network boundaries, and establishes routing across the internet.
notes: also known as: internet protocol; bgp; ip address; router; protocol | see also: internet protocol; bgp; ip address; router; protocol

definition: Internet Protocol address. A number assigned to each device that is sending or receiving data across the internet or a LAN.
Internet Protocol Security. A suite of protocols that secures data sent over an IP network, often used to establish VPNs.
notes: also known as: internet protocol security; vpn | see also: internet protocol security; vpn

definition: Internet Service Provider. A company that provides individuals and organisations access to the internet, and other related services.
notes: also known as: internet service provider; carrier | see also: internet service provider; carrier

definition: Internet Exchange Point. A location where ISPs exchange data between their networks.
notes: also known as: internet exchange point

definition: Hardware or software used to covertly record each keystroke entered into a computer or device.
notes: see also: end user device; keystroke

definition: A single press of a key on a computer.
Key management system. The processes used to generate, store, share, use and destroy cryptographic keys
Local area network. A collection of computers and devices in the same location (usually a building), connected by ethernet or wifi.
notes: also known as: local area network; wan; wifi | see also: local area network; wan; wifi

definition: Large language models (LLMs) use algorithms trained on a huge amount of data, turning relationships between pieces of data into probabilities to predict sequences of text (or increasingly other content) in response to user prompts.
notes: also known as: llm; ai; artificial intelligence | see also: llm; ai; artificial intelligence

definition: Large language models (LLMs) use algorithms trained on a huge amount of data, turning relationships between pieces of data into probabilities to predict sequences of text (or increasingly other content) in response to user prompts.
notes: also known as: large language model; ai; artificial intelligence | see also: large language model; ai; artificial intelligence

definition: Generating, capturing, retaining and storing information on security-relevant events or behaviour from computer systems.
notes: also known as: security monitoring | see also: security monitoring

definition: Machine learning (ML) is a type of AI by which computers find patterns in data or solve problems automatically without having to be explicitly programmed. Most AI in current use is built using ML techniques.
notes: also known as: ml; ai | see also: ml; ai

definition: A small program that can automate tasks in applications (such as Microsoft 365) which attackers can exploit to cause harm.
An NCSC service that helps public sector organisations improve the security of their email domains.
Using online advertising as a delivery method for malware.
notes: also known as: malicious advertising; malware | see also: malicious advertising; malware

definition: Derived from 'malicious software', malware is any kind of software that can damage computer systems, networks or devices. Includes viruses, ransomware and trojans.
notes: also known as: malicious software; virus | see also: malicious software; virus

definition: Mobile Device Management. Software that simplifies the administration and configuration of smartphones, laptops, tablets and other devices across an organisation.
notes: also known as: mobile device management; devices | see also: mobile device management; devices

definition: Metadata describes the properties of data and communications. Examples include file date and packet size.
Actions that organisations and individuals can take to minimise and address risks.
The analysis of logging information to identify unusual events or patterns of behaviour.
The National Protective Security Authority (NPSA) is the UK’s National Technical Authority for physical and personnel protective security.
The National Cyber Security Centre, the UK national technical authority for cyber security.
notes: also known as: national cyber security centre

definition: An NCSC initiative where startup organisations work with NCSC experts to address important cyber challenges.
Multiple computers and/or devices connected to shared resources and information.
A protocol that allows a 3rd party (for example, Google or Microsoft) to authorise access to services on a user's behalf.
Original Equipment Manufacturer. An organisation that makes hardware or software that's used in other products.
notes: also known as: original equipment manufacturer

definition: When referring to software, software for which the source code is made freely available for use and modification.
The basic software running on computers, tablets and smartphones, required to run additional applications and hardware.
notes: also known as: os; platform | see also: os; platform

definition: The day-to-day protection of computer systems from cyber attack. Examples include backup, incident management and patching.
Technology that interfaces with the physical world and includes Industrial Control Systems (ICS), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) and Distributed Control Systems (DCS)
A more secure alternative to passwords that you don't need to remember as they are created and managed safely on your device(s).
A sequence of words or other characters longer than a normal password, used to verify the identity of a user, usually to gain access to an account, website or system.
Software which can generate, store and retrieve passwords.
Using a small number of commonly-used passwords in an attempt to access to a large number of accounts.
A sequence of characters used to verify the identity of a user, usually to gain access to an account, website or system.
Installing updates to devices or software to improve security and/or enhance functionality.
An architectural pattern for secure system design
An authorised test of a computer network or system, designed to look for security weaknesses with the end aim of fixing them.
notes: also known as: pen testing; pentest; vulnerabilities | see also: pen testing; pentest; vulnerabilities

definition: Short for 'penetration testing,' an authorised test of a computer network or system, designed to look for security weaknesses with the end aim of fixing them.
Cyber security that focuses on the behavioural traits of the people who follow the processes put in place by organisations.
Information that relates to an identified or identifiable individual, such as a name or number, or information such as an IP address.
Protecting organisations from the risks associated with the people it employs.
A cyber attack where a person is misdirected to a fraudulent website, despite them entering the correct address.
Scam emails or text messages that contain links to websites which may contain malware, or may trick users into revealing sensitive information (such as passwords) or transferring money.
notes: see also: smishing; spear-phishing; whaling; malware

definition: Protecting the places where people work, and the locations of systems, services and networks.
In cryptography, plain text refers to text that has not been encrypted (and is therefore easily readable).
Hardware or software environments on which applications can run. Software examples include Windows, Android and iOS. Hardware examples include PCs and Mac computers.
A business model that allows developers to create and manage software applications, without having to own or maintain the physical infrastructure on which they run.
notes: see also: infrastructure as a service (iaas); software as a service (saas)

definition: A powerful command-line tool and scripting language, often used to automate administrative and configuration tasks on Windows computers.
A set of rules that determine how data is handled, protected and transmitted between devices on a network.
A type of malware which prevents you from accessing your device and the data stored on it, usually by encrypting your files. A criminal group will then demand a ransom in exchange for decryption.
A business model where consumers purchase ransomware services 'off the shelf', without requiring the technical skills to develop the malware.
notes: also known as: raas; ransomware; malware | see also: raas; ransomware; malware

definition: Working away from your business premises.
notes: also known as: home working; teleworking; byod | see also: home working; teleworking; byod

definition: Devices such as USB flash drives, DVDs and memory cards, that can be easily removed from a computer to increase storage capacity (or to transfer data).
Research & Academia and education
Request for Comments. Detailed proposals concerning an aspect of internet technology, many of which become internet standards.
notes: also known as: request for comments

definition: Radio frequency identification. Technology that uses electromagnetic fields to identify, track and share data between tags within objects or worn by people.
notes: also known as: radio frequency identification

definition: Possible future outcomes that we can describe in terms of their chances of occurrence, and the impact they would have if realised.
The level of risk that an organisation is prepared to take in pursuit of its objectives.
The means of governing, communicating and making decisions about risk.
notes: see also: risk; risk appetite; risk owner

definition: A person who is accountable for risk within an organisation.
A device which sends data from one network to another based on the destination IP address.
Response policy zone. A filtering mechanism that prevents users from visiting harmful internet sites. Also known as a DNS firewall.
notes: also known as: response policy zone; dns | see also: response policy zone; dns

definition: Random data that is added to the password hashing process to help protect against dictionary attacks.
Techniques that ensure data stored on devices is removed so that it can no longer be recovered by unauthorised parties.
Technology in which security is embedded from its inception, and is frictionless for the user. Security is treated as a core fundamental rather than a follow-up activity.
Activities relating to the design and development of IT systems and software that are secure by default.
Protecting digitally stored information using physical and electronic techniques.
The practice of designing the technology, people and processes within computer systems to achieve security goals.
Active analysis by organisations of logging information to look for signs of attacks or unusual behaviour, to help detect events that could be considered an incident and to respond accordingly.
notes: also known as: protective monitoring; logging; soc; siem | see also: protective monitoring; logging; soc; siem

definition: A software- or hardware-based device that accepts and responds to requests made over a network.
Refers to the unknown assets that are used within an organisation for business purposes.
Observing information (such as a password) as it is being typed into a screen.
Technology that supports threat detection and security management by collecting, monitoring and analysing security-related data from computer logs and alerts.
notes: also known as: security information and event management; logging | see also: security information and event management; logging

definition: Using a single set of credentials (such as the same login and password combination) to access multiple services.
notes: also known as: sso; credentials | see also: sso; credentials

definition: Combination of 'SMS' and 'Phishing'. Untargeted text messages sent to large numbers of people, asking for sensitive information, or encouraging them to visit a fake website.
notes: also known as: sms phishing; phishing | see also: sms phishing; phishing

definition: Security operations centre. A centralised facility within an organisation, responsible for activities such as security monitoring and incident management.
notes: also known as: security operations centre; incident management; security monitoring | see also: security operations centre; incident management; security monitoring

definition: A technique an attacker uses to manipulate people into carrying out specific actions, or divulging information.
notes: see also: phishing; smishing; spear-phishing; whaling

definition: Websites and apps, such as Facebook, X and Instragram, that allow people to share and respond to user-generated content (text posts, photos and video).
Security that accounts for the interaction of technology with people, processes and organisations.
A business model where customers access centrally hosted software applications over the internet (or network).
notes: see also: infrastructure as a service (iaas); platform as a service (paas)

definition: Unsolicited email sent in bulk, also referred to as junk email.
A form of phishing targeting particular individuals, where the email is designed to look like it's from a trusted or known person.
notes: also known as: whaling; phishing | see also: whaling; phishing

definition: A technique attackers use to appear to make their communications appear from a legitimate source. Email addresses, display names and domains can all be spoofed.
A type of malware that installs on a device without the user's consent, collecting data and then sendng it to a third party.
Signalling System 7. An international standard allowing phone networks to exchange the information needed for passing calls and text messages.
Secure Sockets Layer. An older method of encrypting data to communicate across the internet. Replaced by TLS.
notes: also known as: secure sockets layer; encryption; tls | see also: secure sockets layer; encryption; tls

definition: The extended network of trading relationships relied upon to deliver products, systems, and services.
The removal of malware and phishing websites to limit the harm they can cause.
Connecting to a phone using wifi, to share its internet connection.
Information about threats that has been aggregated, analysed and enriched to provide the useful context for decision-making processes.
Forcing a time delay between successive login attempts in order to defend against brute force attacks.
Transport Layer Security. A cryptographic protocol that provides secure communications across computer networks, such as the internet.
notes: also known as: transport layer security; encryption | see also: transport layer security; encryption

definition: A physical device or digital data, typically used with a password, to provide a user with access to a computer or network.
A branch of security monitoring designed to protect online systems and services.
A type of malware, disguised as legitimate software, that is used to gain unauthorised access to a victim's computer.

A technique used by threat actors where they set up a website or software with a misspelling of something legitimate. This can trick a user who accidentally misspells a service into entering information into the malicious website or downloading malware.
notes: also known as: typosquatting; attacker; hacker; cyber attack | see also: typosquatting; attacker; hacker; cyber attack

definition: Unified Extensible Firmware Interface. A replacement for the BIOS (Basic Input-Output System) firmware that connects a PC's vital components.
notes: also known as: unified extensible firmware interface; bios; firmware | see also: unified extensible firmware interface; bios; firmware

definition: UK GDPR is the General Data Protection legislation in the UK that gives citizens more control over their personal data.
notes: also known as: general data protection regulation

definition: A live audio and video conversation between two or more people, conducted using phone, tablet, laptop or desktop computer.
A virtual environment within a physical computer that behaves like a separate computer. It allows the user to run programs and test apps in isolation.
The process of creating a virtual machine.
A type of malware that is designed to infect legitimate software programs and replicates across networks when those programs are activated.
Files containing data unique to particular pieces of malware, including viruses, that security software downloads in order to aid detection.
Virtual Private Network. A set of cryptographic technologies used to encrypt data as it travels over a network between two fixed endpoints.
A weakness, or flaw, in software, a system or process. An attacker exploits these to (for example) gain unauthorised access to a computer system.
The World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) is an international organisation that develops standards for the World Wide Web.
notes: also known as: world wide web consortium; www | see also: world wide web consortium; www

definition: Wide area network. A network spread over a large area, typically a collection of Local Area Networks (LANs) and other networks connected together
notes: also known as: wide area network; lan | see also: wide area network; lan

definition: The name of a ransomware variant that in 2017 infected over 300,000 computers globally causing multiple cyber attacks.
Wireless access point. A device which provides wireless access to a network.
notes: also known as: wireless access points; network | see also: wireless access points; network

definition: A type of cyber attack where an attacker identifies a website frequented by a particular group of users, and then compromises that website with malware to infect the computers of its visitors.
notes: also known as: watering hole attack; download attack | see also: watering hole attack; download attack

definition: An NCSC service that helps owners of public sector websites to find and fix common vulnerabilities.
Highly targeted phishing attacks (that look like legitimate emails) aimed at senior executives.
Wireless network technology used to connect devices to the internet (or LAN).
A destructive type of malware that destroys the data on the computers and networks it infects.
A type of malware, similar to a virus, which self-replicates across networks without the need for a user to open an infected program.
The World Wide Web (or Web) is an interconnected system of public webpages accessed through the internet.
notes: also known as: world wide web; internet; browser | see also: world wide web; internet; browser

definition: Recently discovered vulnerability that hackers can explot, where a fix or patch isn't publicly available yet.
2-step verification. The use of two different methods (such as a password and an SMS code) to verify a user's identity.
notes: also known as: 2sv; multi-factor authentication (mfa); two-factor authentication (2fa); authentication; passwords | see also: 2sv; multi-factor authentication (mfa); two-factor authentication (2fa); authentication; passwords

definition: 2-step verification. The use of two different methods (such as a password and an SMS code) to verify a user's identity.
notes: also known as: 2 step verification; two factor authentication; multi-factor authentication; 2-step verification; authentication | see also: 2 step verification; two factor authentication; multi-factor authentication; 2-step verification; authentication

definition: 5G is the fifth generation of wireless technology being rolled out in the UK, used to deliver the mobile internet without connecting to a cable or wifi. 5G is faster than the previous 3G and 4G generations, with reduced latency (the time between instructing a wireless device to perform an action and that action being completed) and greater capacity, allowing more devices in one area to be connected at the same time.
